cards.text: When passing a std::vector to a function that doesn't modify it, pass by {{c1::const reference}}: {{c2::const std::vector<int>&}}}
Avoid passing std::vector by {{c1::value}} because it creates a {{c2::copy}} of the entire vector. Instead, pass by {{c3::reference}} to avoid copying.

cards.question: What's the best way to write this function signature?
cards.explanation: Pass by const reference to avoid copying the vector. The function doesn't modify v, so const is appropriate.

cards.text: When passing a std::vector to a function that needs to modify it, pass by {{c1::non-const reference}}: {{c2::std::vector<int>&}}

cards.concept: Best practices for passing std::vector to functions
cards.explanation:
Follow these guidelines:
  • Pass by const reference for read-only access
  • Pass by non-const reference to modify
  • Never pass by value (expensive copy)
  • Return by value when creating a new vector (move semantics)

cards.text: It's {{c1::rarely}} acceptable to pass std::vector by value. Only do so when you need a {{c2::copy}} to modify locally without affecting the {{c3::original}}.

cards.question: What will this output?
cards.explanation: v is passed by reference and modified in addOne. The first element changes from 1 to 2.

cards.text: In a range-based for loop, to modify vector elements use {{c1::int&}} (reference). To read-only, use {{c2::const int&}} or just {{c3::int}} (for cheap-to-copy types).
When passing a std::vector<int> with 1000 elements by value, all {{c1::1000 integers}} must be {{c2::copied}} into a new vector. Passing by {{c3::reference}} avoids this cost.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Returns a reference to a local variable that will be destroyed. This is a dangling reference.
cards.explanation: v is destroyed when the function returns. The reference points to destroyed memory. Return by value instead.

cards.text: Use const std::vector<T>& as a parameter when the function needs to {{c1::read}} the vector but {{c2::doesn't need to modify}} it. This is the {{c3::most common}} case for vector parameters.
